title: SEGMENT 8 — Antisense oligonucleotides — what they are and how they work

lines.text: SEGMENT 8 — Antisense oligonucleotides — what they are and how they work
Alright.
We have, hopefully, set up the problem.
Now let's talk about the tool.
An antisense oligonucleotide — usually shortened to ASO — is, mechanically, an extremely simple kind of drug.
It is a short piece of synthetic nucleic acid, typically between fifteen and twenty-five bases long, designed to be the exact reverse complement — the mirror image — of a chosen target RNA sequence.

The idea is that because of Watson-Crick base pairing — A pairs with T or U, G pairs with C — a mirror-image piece of nucleic acid will find and bind to its target RNA inside the cell, sequence-specifically, with very high precision.
Once it is bound, depending on the design, the ASO can do one of several things to that target RNA.
It can recruit an enzyme called RNase H to chop the target up.
It can simply sit there and prevent the target from being read by the ribosome.
Or — and this is the case we care about for milasen — it can mask a sequence motif that some piece of the cellular machinery would otherwise have recognized.
Here is the analogy I want you to use, and I want you to hold this in your head for the rest of the lecture.
An antisense oligonucleotide is a sticky note that finds and silences a specific RNA sequence.
Imagine you are reading a long sentence, and someone places a small sticky note over one word in that sentence.
Your eye skips over the covered word.
You read the rest of the sentence without it.

The word is still there, on the page, underneath the sticky note — nothing has been removed, nothing has been chemically changed — but your reading process has effectively skipped that word, because it was covered.
That is, conceptually, what an ASO does.

It is a piece of synthetic mirror-image RNA — or really, a chemically modified analog of RNA, which we'll get to — that finds one specific sequence in one specific transcript inside a cell and lays itself down on top of that sequence, covering it.

The cellular machinery that was supposed to recognize that sequence — the ribosome, or a splicing factor, or RNase H, depending on what kind of ASO it is — either can't get to its target because the sticky note is in the way, or interprets the sticky-note-plus-target as a different kind of substrate and does something different in response.
Now, two practical points about ASOs as drugs that you need to know.
First, you cannot put a piece of normal, unmodified RNA into a person and expect it to do anything useful.
The bloodstream is full of enzymes called RNases that chew up RNA in seconds.
Even if you got it past the RNases, normal RNA does not cross cell membranes easily, and inside cells it gets degraded fast.

So for ASOs to be useful as drugs, the chemistry of the nucleic acid backbone has to be modified to make the molecule resistant to nucleases, to improve its uptake into cells, and to tune its binding properties.

The modifications that have become standard for therapeutic ASOs include replacing the natural phosphodiester linkages between bases with phosphorothioate linkages — where a sulfur atom replaces one of the oxygens — which makes the molecule nuclease-resistant and improves cellular uptake.

And modifications to the sugar component of each nucleotide, the most common of which for splice-modulating ASOs is 2'-O-methoxyethyl, abbreviated 2'-MOE, which tightens binding to the target RNA and further protects from degradation.
If those chemistry words are washing over you, don't worry about memorizing them.
The point is that a clinical ASO is not just naked RNA.
It is RNA whose chemical backbone has been carefully engineered, base by base, to survive in the body, get into cells, find its target, and stick to it.

The chemistry of those modifications was worked out over decades, primarily by a single company called Ionis Pharmaceuticals — formerly called Isis, until they changed their name for obvious reasons — and that chemistry is the platform on top of which essentially all modern therapeutic ASOs are built.
Second practical point: ASOs do not cross the blood-brain barrier when injected into the bloodstream.
The blood-brain barrier is the tight junction system that lines the blood vessels of the brain and keeps most large molecules — including ASOs — out of the central nervous system.
So if your target is a gene expressed in neurons, you cannot just give the ASO by IV and expect it to reach the brain.

You have to inject it directly into the cerebrospinal fluid — the fluid that bathes the brain and spinal cord — by inserting a needle into the lower back, between the vertebrae, into the space surrounding the spinal cord.
That route is called intrathecal injection.
It is exactly the same procedure as a spinal tap, except instead of pulling fluid out, you are pushing drug in.
Here is the analogy.
Intrathecal injection is going through the lower back into the cerebrospinal fluid, where the drug bathes the brain and spinal cord directly.

The CSF circulates throughout the central nervous system, and an ASO injected into the lumbar CSF will distribute, over hours to days, to the brain and spinal cord, where it can enter neurons and do its work.
So to summarize this segment.
An ASO is a short, chemically modified piece of synthetic mirror-image nucleic acid that binds to a specific RNA target by Watson-Crick base pairing.
Depending on its design, it can destroy the target, block its translation, or cover up a sequence motif within it.
To get an ASO into the central nervous system, you inject it intrathecally — directly into the cerebrospinal fluid by lumbar puncture.
The chemistry of the molecule has been engineered over decades to make it stable, deliverable, and effective.
Now — for milasen specifically, the kind of ASO we want is the masking kind.
The covering-up kind.
We want to put a sticky note on the cryptic splice site that the SVA introduced into MFSD8 intron 6, so that the spliceosome stops recognizing it and goes on to use the real exon 7 acceptor instead.

That class of ASO is called a splice-switching or splice-modulating ASO, and it has a particular history of its own — including a critical precedent drug, already FDA-approved at the time of Mila's case, that we will discuss next.
